timeline.event: Born in Leipzig as Clara Josephine Wieck.
Her parents divorced.
Made her official debut at the Gewandhaus in Leipzig.
Met Robert Schumann.
Composed Four Polonaises for Piano, Op. 1.
Toured Paris and other European cities with her father.
Performed her Piano Concerto in A minor with the Leipzig Gewandhaus Orchestra conducted by Mendelssohn.
Accepted Robert Schumann's marriage proposal.
Appointed 'Royal and Imperial Austrian Chamber Virtuoso', Austria's highest musical honor.
Married the composer Robert Schumann.
Collaborated with Robert Schumann to publish a work based on Friedrich Rückert's poem cycle 'Liebesfrühling' (Spring of Love).
Met violinist Joseph Joachim for the first time.
Premiered Robert Schumann's Piano Concerto in Dresden.
Composed Piano Trio in G minor, Op. 17.
Rescued her children during the May Uprising in Dresden.
Met Johannes Brahms.
Composed Three Romances for Violin and Piano, Op. 22.
Robert Schumann suffered a mental breakdown, attempted suicide, and was admitted to an asylum.
Toured England for the first time.
Her husband Robert Schumann died.
Gave the first performance of Brahms' piano solo work, Variations and Fugue on a Theme by Handel.
Continued her sixteen annual winter-spring tours of England.
Worked on arrangements of 41 of Robert Schumann's lieder commissioned by a publisher.
Appointed as the first piano teacher at the newly founded Dr. Hoch's Konservatorium in Frankfurt.
Performed Beethoven's Choral Fantasy with the newly founded Berlin Philharmonic Orchestra.
Edited her husband Robert Schumann's letters (Jugendbriefe).
Edited her husband Robert Schumann's piano works by adding fingerings and other instructions.
Gave her last performance in Frankfurt.
Maintained her teaching position.
Passed away at the age of 76.
A portrait created by Andreas Staub in 1835 was featured on the 100 Deutsche Mark banknote.

keyAchievements.title: Renowned Concert Pianist
keyAchievements.description: Considered one of the most distinguished pianists of the Romantic era, she exerted her influence through a 61-year concert career.

keyAchievements.title: Innovator of Piano Recital Format
keyAchievements.description: She changed the format and repertoire of piano recitals by reducing the importance of purely virtuosic works, and made playing from memory the standard for concerts.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Composer
keyAchievements.description: She composed works in various genres, including piano solo pieces, piano concertos, chamber music, choral works, and lieder.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Piano Educator
keyAchievements.description: From 1878, she worked as an influential piano educator at Dr. Hoch's Konservatorium in Frankfurt, attracting international students.

keyAchievements.title: Editor of Robert Schumann's Works
keyAchievements.description: She edited the publications of her husband Robert Schumann's works, and added instructions to his letters and piano works.

keyAchievements.title: Promoter of Robert Schumann's Music
keyAchievements.description: Throughout her life, she tirelessly promoted her husband Robert Schumann's works to ensure they were recognized and appreciated.

faq.question: Who was Clara Schumann?
faq.answer: Clara Schumann was a German pianist, composer, and piano teacher, evaluated as one of the most distinguished pianists of the Romantic era.

faq.question: When did Clara Schumann marry Robert Schumann?
faq.answer: Clara Schumann married the composer Robert Schumann on September 12, 1840.

faq.question: How did Clara Schumann influence piano performance?
faq.answer: She made playing from memory the standard for concerts, and changed the format and repertoire of piano recitals by reducing the importance of purely virtuosic works.

faq.question: When did Clara Schumann die?
faq.answer: Clara Schumann died on May 20, 1896, at the age of 76, and was buried next to her husband Robert Schumann in Bonn.

faq.question: Is there a banknote featuring Clara Schumann's portrait?
faq.answer: Yes, Clara Schumann's portrait, created by Andreas Staub in 1835, was used on the 100 Deutsche Mark banknote from 1989 to 2002.
